speaker: interviewer
text: [? Phyllis ?] Dreazen is talking with Helen Zelinger. So you came to the United States with your husband, of whom your parents approved when they met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, no question.

speaker: interviewer
text: They liked him better than the other guys who had brought home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, very much. I think he was very passionate at the time. He was very gentle. And I think he missed the family surroundings that he lost, and didn't have it. So he found here people who cared, who loved him. And that was very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you talk with your parents about this guy and your various guys? How did they let you know that they approved of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just let me know with their no response at all. Because previously, if some other friend came, they said, 'Well, we don't like him' or whatever. And when I introduced him, and he came over, I mean, my mother made a beautiful dinner. And that was the end of it. So it was approval. I didn't have to ask her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you had said the story with the blanket turning into the elegant coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talked about your dress. What about your ring, your wedding ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's a little story behind it. When I met Bernie, actually, he was a student. And he was-- he was no money. He lived by a German woman, a family, who always accepted him and handled him like their own child. They didn't have any children. When the committee [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] assigned him to get that room, he knocked at the door. That's what he told me, the story. And he said to the-- her name was [? Anna ?] Gemeiner, [? Gemeiner. ?] He said to the lady, 'If you're going to be good to me, I'll be good to you.' He walked in with one pair of shoes, with one shirt, one pair of underwear. And they accepted him as their own child. They were very nice to him. They never ate a meal if he wasn't back from school. She used to wash his hair. She washed his clothes by hand. And one day, when he met me-- she was the authority-- he said, 'You come over to my apartment.' We didn't live far from each other. He said, 'I would like to introduce you.' And I was lucky. She approved. So at the wedding, she felt like family. All the years being in the United States, we were in touch with her. She had the pictures from our children. We all knew what's going on with her. The last time we saw her was during the Olympics in 1972. We came to Germany. We stayed in the same room. When I-- when I got married, she gave us an extra room. So we had two rooms by her. And we bought tickets for the Olympics for her. And in German, she said to us, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. She would never dream this in her life, that she'd be able to go. The food was so sparely in her place that I said, 'You know, let's go shopping and take some sandwiches. Because at the Olympics, it's going to be very difficult. It's busy.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came with her to the deli, which was just down from her home-- from her apartment she didn't have a home. She asked, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] means 'three slices.' I said, 'Honey, the times are over. We're going to take in each roll three slices for each of us.' So she was a great lady. Being there, married, I had children already. She still-- we couldn't stay in a hotel. We had to be with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She washed our clothes still while we were there. And in the afternoon, or we went out and came back, it was on the dresser like the years of college-- already ironed, put together. So these were the years in Munich with [? Anna ?] Gemeiner, and her husband, and her sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she help your husband find a ring for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she did not. Then when I-- when we got closer and thinking a wedding is going to be, and who's going to buy the ring, Bernie told me that he has five gold-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call it now? I can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Coins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coins, and he would like to make some wedding bands from it. I agreed, because I thought that's-- I mean, it's his contribution. It's just lovely. But I said, 'You know, it's great.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to a jeweler. He gave him the coin, and he created two bands for us. The rest of it, I didn't want him to spend anything. I said, 'This is-- we have to save. We might need it.'

speaker: interviewee
text: But our parents helped us out. My brother used to come by train from Landsberg to Munich almost every Saturday with a Saturday cooked meal, with the pots and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you went to Milwaukee in 1950, your brother was there also, and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to work as a dentist?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not work as a dentist, but I worked as a dental hygienist those days in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Milwaukee. His name was [? Jack ?] Weiss. I just didn't work as a dentist. I went from one chair-- he started to work, and I finished it. And he used me a lot in his laboratory to make crowns and bridges. Whatever I could, I did. And one-- I was paid very little. I was paid $55 a week for all the work I did. But one day, I asked him, because I did more than that-- not one day, maybe after a year or whatever, I don't remember, if he could give me a raise. He said I should leave the keys. Which I left, and I found another job. I was then pregnant with my son. [? Richardson ?] was his name. I loved every drop of work that he did. That was real dentistry. I stayed with him only several months, unfortunately, because I didn't feel good through the pregnancy. And this was the end of my dentistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Milwaukee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milwaukee, we lived there-- Bernie did his internship in Milwaukee. Of course, I didn't tell you the story, and I won't. It's too long. He got sick in New York. He had the mumps. We didn't have no family, nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he decided he would like to move to Milwaukee, where my parents are. My mother negotiated the whole situation with the-- with the president, the director of the hospital, and they accepted him. So we stayed with my mother that year. I lived with my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember how the story came about. You asked me something here. How long were we in Milwaukee. We were in Milwaukee-- I was in Milwaukee longer, because Bernie, after his internship-- not internship-- after his residency, was taken to Korea. He was a doctor in Korea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had Allan, who's my son. He was 16 months old. And I went back to live with my parents until he came back. How long was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Korea 18 months. And when he came back, he was in Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, which I could join him. Then we shipped all out to Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri-- myself, my son, and my husband. And there were precious months that he had to finish, to 24 months. Our son got to know his father. And that was a real honeymoon for me. We had our-- he was an officer, so we had our own little home. And it was just beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, they took him into service when he was already a resident. After that, he came back to Chicago. I moved to Chicago. He did his pathology residency at Michael Reese Hospital. And we lived close, on Prairie Avenue, close to the hospital. He did pathology six months, and then he went to finish the rest to Mount Sinai Hospital. Then I lived shortly close to Mount Sinai. Then I lived in-- in Hometown, Illinois, because I didn't like the surroundings by Mount Sinai. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the section, actually, where he established his office, after he graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: South?

speaker: interviewee
text: South.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his office was?

speaker: interviewee
text: His first office was on Cicero, Cicero and Pulaski.

speaker: interviewer
text: In pathology?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a gynecologist already, see? When he came back, he-- from the army, he finished his residency. At the time, we had a lot of money. He used to be paid $175 a month, because they took him away. So the government gave him $175, and the hospital paid $150. If you can add, that's a lot of money. So we-- he smoked at the time, and he could afford the smoking. And I could afford buying a little clothes. And the standards were fine. I was happy. I was very happy those days. I knew the future was going to be brighter. But we were very happy, what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents stayed in Milwaukee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was with the brewery?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My father had a very short time with the brewery. He developed asthma.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he worked for certain department store. There used to be a Boston department store, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in men's furnishing. Then he had all kinds of different jobs, because he couldn't be in the brewery no more. They brought him home several times with emergency asthma attacks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they lived in Milwaukee until-- my mother just lived there until three years ago. I brought her here to a retirement home, at The Breakers on Gulf Road. And my mother's going to be 90 years old this August. My father passed away 17 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Allan grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Allan grew up a healthy, nice guy. We had a lot of pleasure, a lot of joy. Then came another little life to-- to our life, which was our daughter when Bernie came back from Korea. And she was born in 1955. And she, naturally, was a delight. We gave the kids the best, what we could those days. They had private schools. I was available at home at the time-- if it was good or not, I don't know. Too much, maybe, is not good, either. But they're now married. They have lovely families. It seems they're very happy. And we are happy with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you say to them about this whole experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children-- I guess my son and my daughter-in-laws, what we went through-- I would say to my grandchildren that they have to be proud living in this country of freedom. Everything is available. They should only know that they are Jews, because I was born a Jew, I suffered as a Jew, and we still suffer as a Jew. So they should carry out the Jewish tradition, and know what the culture and being Jewish means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your experience affected how you brought them up, your being available to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have mixed emotions. I really have mixed emotions. Knowing now the way it's going on, you see, at the time, my husband was very important. Because he was the one who [? sat ?] in the book and studied for his board. I couldn't afford a nanny. I couldn't afford anybody. So I had to be with the kids available. And you get involved with the kids, and then you forget. You know, it took quite a few years until we could afford a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was actually brought up European. I had my pride. Because I think, on my own, I accomplished a lot, with not even guidance, after going through what I went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: But schooling was very important for me. I was good in it. I was a great dentist. The professors used to-- if they have a difficult case at university, they used to send to [? Helen ?] Renski, which I was proud of it. But the circumstances in the States-- I think my husband would blame me. 'If I would want to, I could have done it.' But it's easy said. I had two young children. I was there with them. That's the culture from Europe what I got. And that's what I did. They have to be the judges and tell if it was wrong for them or not, if I gave too much time. We were devoted parents. They shared with us. We were there when they needed us. And that's all what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like a wonderful life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing with us some stories of it. Thank you from the [? Spielberg ?] Foundation. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much, also. And this picture-- this picture was taken in DP camp in Austria. And as you see to the left, that's me. And to the right is my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a DP camp, a transit camp, where we were waiting to be shipped to Germany. This picture is a picture from my wedding-- actually, the wedding day. You see myself and my husband. There's my mother, Lola, my father, Mayer, my brother-- the only brother, Henry. That was taken in Germany, in Landsberg. As you see, the dress-- the dress was bought. I was very proud at the time that my parents could afford and buy me a dress. And this is the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was March 20th, 1949. This is my husband. It's the day of the wedding. And this is the coat-- the story that I told you about the coat, and hat, and the suit, and the shoes. He's standing in Munich in front of the-- of the destroyed train station, ready to come to Landsberg. As I told you before that my husband had five gold coins. And we took one coin, went to the jeweler, and made us the two wedding bands.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they say on the inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, it's engraved, 'HZ,' which means Helen Zelinger, the date, 'March 20th, '39.' And on his, inside, it says, 'BZ, March 20th, '39.'

speaker: interviewee
text: '49, I apologize. As I told you before about my-- my experience in dentistry, this picture shows you my practice on the ship we came to United States, [? Genoma ?] Gray. Basically, I took care of the sailors, because they had problems. This is me working in his mouth. And this is a lady who she claimed she was a nurse before the war, and she was my assistant. This picture was given to us from my daughter and our son-- son-in-law, our son. It was my 65th birthday. And this is my mother in the center, it's me, my lovely daughter, and my two lovely grandchildren. It's Lucy, Lauren, Arielle, Lola and Helen. And this picture, it's taken a few years ago. But he cannot be with us today, unfortunate. So it's my daughter and our son, Richard and Lucy. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] Keep on going, guys. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Helen, who is this-- who are the people with you gathered around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who are the people? Next to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you introduce us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me to my right is our son, Allan. Next to Allan is his wife, Caryn, his daughter Marissa. And in front is Bradley, our grandson. To my left is my husband. And to his left is our daughter, Lucy. Behind are her two daughters, Arielle and-- and Lauren. We don't have our son-in-law. He had to go on a convention. So I do-- his name is Richard-- I do hope that we have the opportunity to make a picture where he's going to be with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to your mom on the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say without crying first?

speaker: interviewer
text: Crying is--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the wrong person to ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: Crying is OK. Allan, do you want to go first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here we are, all of us. We've multiplied. We're all in one piece. We wouldn't be here if they didn't make it here from-- through the war, and through all the activities that occurred, luck, miracles, and some blessings from above. And thank God we're all healthy and here, and sitting together for this occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Caryn?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're glad to be here as a family unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I think I can try. It's-- it's a very emotional thing for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK, you can do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just try.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I think it's wonderful that we're here, and we're preserving the stories and the memories for us, and for generations to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up with the stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up with some of the stories. And over the past two years, I think it's wonderful that this is being done, as a child of a survivor. Because I've been bothering my father who probably, I think, has held more back over the years than my mother has, to tell me I wanted to do this to him in order to get his story out. Because I told him for years, if anybody asks me or I had to repeat his history chronologically, I could never do it. Because I would hear a story here, a story there, an episode here, an episode there. And I was very interested for my own self to know what the history of my family was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think this is wonderful. And we had been working on-- he's been dictating his stories, and I've been typing them. And this is a nice conclusion to the whole thing. Grandchildren, what would you like to say to your grandma? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's very nice, and I love her a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Same with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bradley?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing!

speaker: interviewer
text: And Helen, what would you say to them, for them to remember? And why do you think that you've survived to come to this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you're asking me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would love to say to all of them-- and especially the young adults, they know it-- but to my grandchildren, that they live in the most beautiful country in the world. They live in a country of freedom. They live in a country of opportunity. They can become whatever they want. I don't think they appreciate this age where they are and what their parents do. But I think their parents are doing as much for them as we did for our children. And I wish you all good health. Do preserve the Jewish tradition, because I, and Grandpa, and a lot of more millions of people, were born Jews, we suffered as Jews, and we have a tremendous tradition that you children should keep with you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And preserve.

speaker: interviewee
text: And preserve.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bernie, what would you say to your lovely bride?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it took me a long time to come to this point. It was effort, luck. And we lived about 46 years together. We've been through a lot. We've built a family. And I'm happy to be at this point in my already advanced age to see being surrounded with some future generations which will carry on our traditions, which I'm very much interested in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last word from anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye-bye!

speaker: interviewer
text: Allan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing more. I think it's all been said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except bye-bye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you from the Shoah Foundation. Thank you for sharing your story. Thank you for being with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
